card.intuition: Unlike 0/1 knapsack, you can take fractions of items. The greedy strategy — sort by value/weight ratio, take as much of the best-ratio item as fits, then the next — is provably optimal because partial items are allowed.
card.analogy: Filling a backpack with gold, silver, and copper dust: you pour in as much of the most valuable metal as the bag holds, then switch to the next. Since you can pour partial amounts, greedy is always optimal.
card.time_complexity: O(n log n) — dominated by sorting
when_to_use: Resource allocation where fractional amounts are possible
When the problem explicitly allows partial items
gotchas: Greedy ONLY works when fractions are allowed — 0/1 knapsack requires DP
Sort by value/weight in DESCENDING order — highest ratio first
Take min(remaining_capacity, item_weight) of each item

exercise.prompt: Implement fractional knapsack using a greedy value/weight ratio sort.
exercise.starter_code:
def fractional_knapsack(weights, values, capacity):
    """
    Returns maximum value (float) achievable with fractional items allowed.
    """
    items = sorted(zip(values, weights),
                   key=lambda x: ___________,   # sort by ratio descending
                   reverse=True)
    total = 0.0
    remaining = capacity
    for val, wt in items:
        if remaining <= 0:
            break
        take = min(remaining, wt)
        total += take * (val / wt)
        remaining -= take
    return total
hints: Sort by the value-to-weight ratio: key=lambda x: x[0] / x[1] — higher ratio = better value per unit weight
exercise.test_code:
import math
result = fractional_knapsack([10,20,30],[60,100,120], 50)
assert math.isclose(result, 240.0), f'Got {result}'  # take all 30, all 20, 10/10 of 10 -> 120+100+20=240
assert fractional_knapsack([5],[10], 3) == 6.0  # 3/5 * 10 = 6
assert fractional_knapsack([1],[1], 0) == 0.0
assert fractional_knapsack([2,2],[4,4], 3) == 6.0  # 1.5 full items
print('PASS')
